title: Dimensional Analysis and its Applications
summary: Dimension of force, $ F = [MLT^{-2}] $ Dimension of velocity, $ v = [LT^{-1}] $ and dimension of time, T = [T] According to the question, $$ [\mathbf{M}]=[\mathbf{F}^{a}\,\mathbf{v}^{b}\,\mathbf{T}^{c}] $$ Where $a$,…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Given $ V = \frac{Px^{2}}{\sqrt{x} + Q} $ Dimension of potential energy = $ [ML^{2}T^{-2}] $ x has dimension of length = [L] $$ \sqrt{x}={\mathbf{L}}^{1/2} $$ $$ \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{L}^{\prime\frac{1}{2}} $$ ( $ \because $ having same dimension of $ (x $) So, now eqn (i) is $$ \mathbf{V}=\frac{\mathbf{P}x^{2}}{\mathbf{L}^{\prime}^{2}+\mathbf{L}^{\prime}^{2}} $$ Dimension P is $ [ML^{2}T^{-2}][L^{-3/2}] $ $ = [ML^{1/2}T^{-2}] $ Now for $ \frac{P^{2}}{Q} $ As, $ Q = L^{\frac{1}{2}} $ $$ \mathrm{\frac{[ML^{\frac{\nu}{2}}T^{-2}]^{2}}{L^{\frac{\nu}{2}}}}\ \Rightarrow\mathrm{\frac{M^{2}L T^{-4}}{L^{\frac{\nu}{2}}}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{P}^{2}}{\mathbf{Q}}=[\mathbf{M}^{2}\mathbf{L}^{\frac{1}{2}}\mathbf{T}^{-4}] $$ ##### ! Caution Students are often confused while writing the units. For this, write the formula first and then its corresponding units. In this way, one can easily calculate the unit. - $$ \mathrm{F}=\alpha t^{2}+\beta b $$ For $$ \mathrm{F}=\alpha t^{2} $$ $$ [\mathrm{M L T}^{-2}]=\alpha[\mathrm{T}^{2}] $$ $$ \alpha=[\mathrm{M L T^{-4}}] $$ For $$ \mathbf{F}=\boldsymbol{\beta t} $$ $$ [{\mathrm{M L T^{-2}}}]=\upbeta[{\mathrm{T}}] $$ $$ \boldsymbol{\beta}=[\mathrm{M L T}^{-3}] $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\alpha t}{\beta}&=\frac{[MLT^{-4}][T]}{[MLT^{-3}]}\\&=[M^{0}L^{0}T^{0}]\end{aligned} $$ - $ L = [c]^x [G]^y \left[ \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \right]^z $ Since coulomb's force is $ F = \frac{e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{2}} $ $$ \frac{e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\;=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{3}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\right] $$ L = [LT^{-1}]^{x} [M^{-1}L^{3}T^{-2}]^{y} [ML^{3}T^{-2}]^{z} $$ [\mathbf{L}]=[\mathbf{L}^{x}+3y+3z\mathbf{M}^{-y}+z\mathbf{T}^{-x}-2y-2z] $$ Comparing both sides, $$ -y+z=0 $$ $$ y=z $$ $$ x+3y+3z=1 $$ $$ -x-4z=0 $$ From (i), (ii) and (iii), $$ z=y=\frac{1}{2},x=-2 $$ Hence, $$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{1}{c^{2}}\left[\mathrm{G}.\frac{e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} $$ - According to question. $$ \mathbf{L}=[h]^{a}\mathbf{\Sigma}[c]^{b}\mathbf{\Sigma}[\mathbf{G}]^{c} $$ Putting the dimensions on both sides, we get, $$ [\mathbf{M}^{0}\mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}^{0}]=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{2}\mathbf{T}^{-1}]^{a}\;[\mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}^{-1}]^{b}\;[\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{L}^{3}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]^{c} $$ $$ [\mathbf{M}^{0}\mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}^{0}]=[\mathbf{M}^{a-c}]\;\left[\mathbf{L}^{2a+b+3c}\right]\;[\mathbf{T}^{-a-b-2c}] $$ Comparing powers on both sides, we get. $$ a-c=0 $$ $$ 2a+b+3c=1 $$ $$ -a-b-2c=0 $$ Solving the above equations, $$ a=c=\frac{1}{2}and b=-\frac{3}{2} $$ Putting these values in eq. (i), we get. $$ \mathrm{L}=[h^{1/2}\ c^{-3/2}\ G^{1/2}]=\frac{\sqrt{h\mathrm{G}}}{c^{3/2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{\sqrt{h\mathrm{G}}}{c^{3/2}} $$ - Dimension of force, $ F = [MLT^{-2}] $ Dimension of velocity, $ v = [LT^{-1}] $ and dimension of time, T = [T] According to the question, $$ [\mathbf{M}]=[\mathbf{F}^{a}\,\mathbf{v}^{b}\,\mathbf{T}^{c}] $$ Where $a$, $b$ and $c$ are constant putting dimensions. $$ [\mathbf{M}]=[\mathbf{M L T}^{-2}]^{a}\;[\mathbf{L T}^{-1}]^{b}[\mathbf{T}]^{c} $$ $$ [\mathbf{M}]=[\mathbf{M}^{a}\,\mathbf{L}^{a+b}\,\mathbf{T}^{-2a-b+c}] $$ Comparing powers on both side, we get: $$ a=1,a+b=0,-2a-b+c=0 $$ Solving the above equation, we get $$ a=1,b=-1\text{and}c=1 $$ Putting these values in eq. (i), we get $$ \mathbf{M}=[\mathbf{F}\mathbf{v}^{-1}\mathbf{T}] $$
##### related Theory Alternative method: $$ F=m a $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ F=\frac{m\mathbf{v}}{t} $$ $$ m=\frac{Ft}{\textbf{v}} $$ $$ M=\frac{[F][T]}{[\mathbf{v}]}=[F\mathbf{v}^{-1}T] $$ - From the formula, $ d_{1} = \frac{m}{V} $ $$ \begin{array}{l}d_{2}=d_{1}\Bigg[\frac{\mathbf{M}_{1}}{\mathbf{M}_{2}}\Bigg]\Bigg[\frac{\mathbf{L}_{1}}{\mathbf{L}_{2}}\Bigg]^{-3}\\=4\Bigg[\frac{1}{100}\Bigg]\Bigg[\frac{1}{10}\Bigg]^{-3}=40\end{array} $$ - This question will be solved with the help of given options: $$X = [M^a L^b T^c]$$ (a) If $a = 1$, $b = -1$, $c = -2$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $X = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}] \rightarrow Pressure$$ (b) If $a = 1$, $b = 0$, $c = -1$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $X = [MT^{-1}]$$ It is not the dimension of velocity. (c) If $a = 1$, $b = 1$, $c = -2$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $X = [MLT^{-2}]$, It is not the dimension of acceleration. (d) If $a = 0$, $b = -1$, $c = -2$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $X = [L^{-1}T^{-2}]$, It is not the dimension of force. - Using principle of homogeniety, $$ \left[\frac{a}{\mathbf{V}^{2}}\right]=\mathbf{P} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}{[a]}&{{}=[\mathbf{P}]\;[\mathbf{V}^{2}]}\\ {}&{{}=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{-1}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]\;[\mathbf{L}^{3}]^{2}}\\ {}&{{}=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{5}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]}\\ \end{aligned} $$ - Using principle of homogeniety. (B) Using principle of homogeniety. Dimension of v = Dimension of at = Dimension of $ \frac{b}{t+c} $ $ \therefore\quad at = [LT^{-1}] $ $ \Rightarrow\quad [a]=\frac{\left[LT^{-1}\right]}{[T]}=[LT^{-2}] $ And c = T \Rightarrow c = [T] And \frac{[b]}{[T]} = [LT^{-1}] \Rightarrow $ [b] = [LT^{-1}] [T] = [L] $ - Force. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{F}&=[MLT^{-2}]\\&=(10\mathrm{~g})(10\mathrm{~cm})(0.1\mathrm{~s})^{-2}\end{aligned} $$ Changing these units into mks system. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{F}&=\left(10^{-2}\mathrm{kg}\right)\left(10^{-1}\mathrm{m}\right)\left(10^{-1}\mathrm{s}\right)^{-2}\\&=10^{-1}\mathrm{N}=0.1\mathrm{~N}\end{aligned} $$ - As. $$ x=a t+b t^{2} $$ According to the concept of dimensional analysis and principle of homogeneity. $$ \because $$ $$ unit~of~x=unit~of~bt^{2} $$ $$ unit of b=\frac{unit of x}{unit of t^{2}}=km/s^{2} $$ - Given, Voltage, $ V=(200\pm4)V $ Current, $ I = (20 \pm 0.2) $ A $$ R=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{200}{20}=10\ \Omega $$ $$ \left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{I}}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \frac{\Delta\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{4}{200}=0.02 $$ $$ \left(\frac{\Delta R}{R}\right)^{2}=(0.02)^{2}+(0.01)^{2} $$ $$ \frac{\Delta R}{R}=\sqrt{0.0005} $$ $$ \Delta\mathbf{R}=\mathbf{R}\times0.0224 $$ $$ \Delta R=0.224 $$ $$ \therefore\mathbf{R}=\left(10\pm0.224\right)\boldsymbol{\Omega}=\left(10\pm0.3\right)\boldsymbol{\Omega} $$ - Random errors are the errors which occur due to random and unpredictable fluctuations in temperature and voltage supply.
##### Related Theory Random errors can arise by some other experimental conditions (Example: u mechanical vibrations of experimental set-ups, etc, errors by the observer taking readings, etc.) - Volume of the wire, $ V = \pi r^{2} L $ Density, $$ \begin{aligned}\rho&=\frac{m}{\mathrm{V}}\\&=\frac{m}{\pi r^{2}\mathrm{L}}\end{aligned}\quad(\because\mathrm{V}=\pi r^{2}\mathrm{L})\quad $$ For % error. $$ \frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho}\times100=\frac{\Delta m}{m}\times100+2\times\frac{\Delta r}{r}\times100+\frac{\Delta L}{L}\times100 $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\therefore\frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho}\times100&=\frac{0.002}{0.4}\times100+2\times\frac{0.001\times100}{0.3}+\frac{0.02}{5}\\&=0.5+0.67+0.4=1.57=1.6\%\end{aligned} $$ $$ \overline{a}=\frac{1.25+1.24+1.27+1.21+1.28}{5} $$

title: Dimensional Formulae and Dimensional Equations
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Dimensional formula of stress = ML^{-1} T^{-2} Dimensional formula of angle = Dimensionless Dimensional formula of strain = Dimensionless Dimensional formula of angular speed = T^{-1} Dimensional formula of arc = L So, strain and angle are dimensionless like solid angle.
- (a)-(ii), (b)-(iv), (c)-(i), (d)-(iii)
- Given. Energy = E Gravitational constant = G We know that. $$ \begin{aligned}[\mathrm{E}]\mathbf{F}&=\mathbf{F.S}\\&=\operatorname{kg}\ \mathbf{m}\mathbf{s}^{-2}\ \mathbf{m}\\&=\operatorname{kg}\ \mathbf{m}^{2}\ \mathbf{s}^{-2}\\&=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{2}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{[G]} =\displaystyle {\frac{F r^{2}}{m^{2}}} \\ \qquad = \displaystyle {\left[ \frac{k \text{g} \text{m s}^{-2} \text{m}^{2}}{k \text{g}^{2}} \right] } \\ \qquad = \displaystyle {\frac{m^{3} \text{s}^{-2}}{k \text{g}}} \\ \qquad = \displaystyle {[\text{M}^{-1} \text{L}^{3} \text{T}^{-2}]} \end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{[E]}{[G]}=&\left[\frac{ML^{2}T^{-2}}{M^{-1}L^{3}T^{-2}}\right]\\=&[M^{1+1}L^{2-3}T^{-2+2}]\\=&[M^{2}L^{-1}T^{0}]\end{aligned} $$
- We know, Given, [E] = [Fα Aβ Tγ] [E] = FS = kg m² s⁻² = [ML²T⁻²] [F] = kg m s⁻² = [MLT⁻²] [A] = ms⁻² = [M⁰LT⁻²] [T] = s = [M⁰L⁰T¹] [ML²T⁻²] = [MLT⁻²]α [LT⁻²]β B[T⁻²] [M¹L²T⁻²] = [Mα Lα⁺β T⁻²α⁻²β⁺γ] On comparing the powers, α = 1 α + β = 2 -2α - 2β + γ = -2 β = 2 - α = 2 - 1 = 1 -2(1) - 2(1) + γ = -2 γ = 2 E = [FAT²]
- [Stress] = $ \frac{[Force]}{[Area]} $ = $ \frac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^{2}]} $ = [ML $ ^{-1} $T $ ^{-2} $]
- Dimension of energy, $ E = [ML^2T^{-2}] $ Dimension of velocity, $ v = [LT^{-1}] $ Dimension of time, $ t = [T] $ From the question, Surface tension, $ S = E a v b T^c $ $$ [S] = [MT^{-2}] $ a, b, c are constants. Equating dimensions on both sides, $$ [\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{0}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{2}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]^{a}\,[\mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}^{-1}]^{b}\,[\mathbf{T}]^{c} $$ $$ [\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{0}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]=[\mathbf{M}^{a}\mathbf{L}^{2a+b}\mathbf{T}^{-2a-b+c}] $$ On comparing both sides, $$ a=1,2a+b=0\text{and}-2a-b+c=-2 $$ On solving these equations, we get, $$ a=1,b=-2,c=-2 $$ Putting the values in eq (i), we get, $$ [\mathbf{S}]=[\mathrm{E v}^{-2}\mathrm{T}^{-2}] $$
- We have, $$ c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}}} $$ So, dimension of $ (\mu_0\varepsilon_0)^{-1/2} $ is equal to the dimension of $ c $. Therefore, dimension of $ c = [LT^{-1}] $.
- Dimension of $ \varepsilon_0 = [\mathrm{M}^{-1}\mathrm{L}^{-3}\mathrm{T}^4\mathrm{A}^2] $ Dimension of $ \mathrm{E} = [\mathrm{MLT}^{-3}\mathrm{A}^{-1}] $ $ \therefore $ Dimension of $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{E}^{2}&=\left[\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathrm{L}^{-3}\mathrm{T}^{4}\mathbf{A}^{2}\right]\left[\mathbf{M}\mathrm{LT}^{-3}\mathbf{A}^{-1}\right]^{2}\\&=\left[\mathbf{M}\mathrm{L}^{-1}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\right]\end{aligned} $$
- Let Let $ h = [p^a d^p] $ $ [ML^2T^{-1}] = [MLT^{-1}]^a [L]^b $ $ [ML^2T^{-1}] = [MaL^{a+bT^{-a}}] $ Comparing powers on both sides, we get $ a = 1, a + b = 2, $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ b = 1 $ Putting these values in eq. (i), we get The dimension of $ h = [pd] $
- Energy density and Young's modulus have same dimension and equal to $ [ML^{-1}T^{-2}] $. Dielectric constant and refractive index are dimension less. #### △! Caution Students should know that the different quantities can have the same dimensional unit. Many students on finding the same dimension of a quantity as of some other quantity cancel out the process and redo the question.
- We know that. $$ \begin{aligned}R&=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{[ML^{2}T^{-2}]}{[I^{2}T]}\\&=[ML^{2}T^{-3}I^{-2}]\end{aligned} $$
- $$ \frac{h}{1}=\frac{E\times\lambda}{c\times I}=\frac{[ML^{2}T^{-2}][L]}{[LT^{-1}][ML^{2}]} $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{h}{I}}=[\mathrm{T}^{-1}] $$ which is the dimension of frequency.
- We have, $ F = \frac{GMm}{R^{2}} $ $$ \begin{array}{l l}{\Rightarrow\quad}&{\mathrm{G}=\frac{\mathrm{F R}^{2}}{\mathrm{M}m}=\frac{[\mathrm{M L T}^{-2}][\mathrm{L}^{2}]}{[\mathrm{M}^{2}]}}\\ {}&{}\\ {}&{=[\mathrm{M}^{-1}\mathrm{L}^{3}\mathrm{T}^{-2}]}\\ \end{array} $$
- $ \frac{L}{R} $ is time constant of R-L circuit so, dimensions of $\frac{L}{R}$ is same as that of time. $$ \frac{\mathrm{D i m e n s i o n s~of~L}}{\mathrm{D i m e n s i o n s~of~R~}}=\frac{[\mathrm{M L}^{2}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-2}]}{[\mathrm{M L}^{2}\mathrm{T}^{-3}\mathrm{A}^{-2}]}=[\mathrm{T}] $$

formulas.latex: [S] = [MT^{-2}] $ a, b, c are constants. Equating dimensions on both sides,
On solving these equations, we get,
Putting the values in eq (i), we get,
S = E a v b T^c

title: Dimensions of Physical Quantities
summary: Magnetic flux, $ \phi = B \cdot A $ As, F = ILB $$ [\mathbf{B}]={\frac{\mathbf{F}}{\mathbf{I L}}}={\frac{\mathbf{M L T}^{-2}}{\mathbf{A L}}}=[\mathbf{M T}^{-2}\mathbf{A}^{-1}] $$ From (i) $$ \phi=[\mathrm{M…
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Magnetic flux, $ \phi = B \cdot A $ As, F = ILB $$ [\mathbf{B}]={\frac{\mathbf{F}}{\mathbf{I L}}}={\frac{\mathbf{M L T}^{-2}}{\mathbf{A L}}}=[\mathbf{M T}^{-2}\mathbf{A}^{-1}] $$ From (i) $$ \phi=[\mathrm{M T}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-1}]\mathrm{L}^{2} $$ $$ [\phi]=[\mathrm{M L^{2}T^{-2}A^{-1}}] $$ Self inductance, $ \phi = LI $ $$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{\phi}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-1}}{\mathrm{A}} $$ $$ [{\mathrm L}]={\mathrm M}{\mathrm L}^{2}{\mathrm T}^{-2}{\mathrm A}^{-2} $$ Magnetic permeability, $ \mu = \frac{B \cdot 2\pi r}{I} $ $$ [\mu]=\frac{[\mathrm{MT}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-1}\mathrm{L}]}{[\mathrm{A}]}=\mathrm{ML}^{1}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-2} $$ Electric permittivity, $ \varepsilon_{0} = \frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{4\pi r^{2}F} $ $$ [\varepsilon_{0}]=[\mathrm{M L}^{-3}\mathrm{T}^{4}\mathrm{A}^{2}] $$
- A quantity which has dimensions and also has a constant value is called dimensional constant. Here, gravitational constant (G) is a dimensional constant.

title: Objective I
summary: Set up the standard right-handed axes used for directions on Earth's surface: $\hat{i} =$ East, $\hat{j} =$ North, $\hat{k} =$ vertically up (this triad satisfies $\hat{i}\times\hat{j}=\hat{k}$).
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- The component of a vector $\vec{A}$ along a direction making angle $\theta$ with it is $A\cos\theta$. Since $-1 \le \cos\theta \le 1$, the component can take **any value from $-A$ to $+A$**: - At $\theta = 0°$: component $= A$ (equals the magnitude) - At $\theta = 90°$: component $= 0$ - At $\theta = 180°$: component $= -A$ So the component is sometimes equal to the magnitude, sometimes less, sometimes zero, and can even be negative — it is never *always* less than (rules out A), never *always* greater than (a component can't exceed the magnitude, so B is impossible too, but "always" makes it false), and never *always* equal (only true for $\theta=0$). Since no single fixed relation holds in every case, the answer is (D) none of these.
- Set up the standard right-handed axes used for directions on Earth's surface: $\hat{i} =$ East, $\hat{j} =$ North, $\hat{k} =$ vertically up (this triad satisfies $\hat{i}\times\hat{j}=\hat{k}$). Given $\vec{A} = A\hat{k}$ (up) and $\vec{B} = B\hat{j}$ (north): $$\vec{A}\times\vec{B} = AB(\hat{k}\times\hat{j})$$ Using the cyclic rule $\hat{i}\times\hat{j}=\hat{k},\ \hat{j}\times\hat{k}=\hat{i},\ \hat{k}\times\hat{i}=\hat{j}$, and that swapping the order reverses the sign: $$\hat{k}\times\hat{j} = -(\hat{j}\times\hat{k}) = -\hat{i}$$ So $\vec{A}\times\vec{B} = -AB\,\hat{i}$, which points in the $-\hat{i}$ direction, i.e., **West**. (You can verify the same with the right-hand rule: curl the fingers of the right hand from "up" towards "north" — the thumb points west.) Answer: (A).
- The radius $r = 2.12$ cm is given to **3 significant figures**. The area is $$A = \pi r^2 = \pi (2.12)^2 = \pi \times 4.4944 = 14.1204\ldots \text{ cm}^2$$ A calculated quantity cannot be more precise than the least precise measurement that produced it — the result must also be quoted to 3 significant figures (the constant $\pi$ and the "squaring" don't add precision beyond what $r$ has). Rounding $14.1204\ldots$ to 3 significant figures gives $$A = 14.1\ \text{cm}^2$$ Option (a) "14 cm²" has only 2 significant figures (under-reports precision), while (c) and (d) keep too many digits (over-stating the precision of the measurement). Answer: (B).
- Pressure = Force / Area. $$[P] = \frac{[\rm Force]}{[\rm Area]} = \frac{\rm MLT^{-2}}{\rm L^2} = [\rm ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$$ This is also the dimension of stress, Young's modulus, and bulk modulus. Answer: (B).
- - Work: $[\rm ML^2T^{-2}]$; Power: $[\rm ML^2T^{-3}]$ — different. - **Torque**: $\vec{\tau}=\vec{r}\times\vec{F}$: $[\rm ML^2T^{-2}]$; **Energy**: $[\rm ML^2T^{-2}]$ — **same**. - Force: $[\rm MLT^{-2}]$; Impulse: $[\rm MLT^{-1}]$ — different. - Linear momentum: $[\rm MLT^{-1}]$; Angular momentum: $[\rm ML^2T^{-1}]$ — different. Although torque and energy share the same dimensions, they are physically distinct (torque is a vector, energy is a scalar). Answer: (B).
- Let $v = k\,T^a\mu^b$. Dimensions: $[\rm LT^{-1}] = [\rm MLT^{-2}]^a[\rm ML^{-1}]^b$. Matching exponents: - M: $0 = a+b \Rightarrow b=-a$ - L: $1 = a-b = a+a = 2a \Rightarrow a=\tfrac12$ - T: $-1 = -2a \Rightarrow a=\tfrac12$ ✓ So $v \propto T^{1/2}\mu^{-1/2} = \sqrt{T/\mu}$. This is the exact correct formula. Answer: (B).
- Significant figures rules: - All non-zero digits are significant. - Zeros between non-zero digits are significant. - Trailing zeros **after** a decimal point are significant. $6.020\times10^{23}$: digits are 6, 0, 2, 0. The zero between 6 and 2 is significant (rule 2), and the trailing zero after 2 is significant (rule 3). Total: **4** significant figures. The exponent ($10^{23}$) does not affect significant figures. Answer: (C).
- Dimensional analysis requires every term to have the same dimensions. However, **trigonometric functions, logarithms, and exponentials are dimensionless** — their arguments must be dimensionless, and their outputs are dimensionless. In $\sin\theta = v/c$, the right side has dimensions $[\rm LT^{-1}/LT^{-1}]$ = dimensionless, and $\sin\theta$ is dimensionless. While the equation may be dimensionally consistent, dimensional analysis cannot verify or derive such a relation (it can only check it for polynomial power-law relations). Answer: (D).

title: Objective II
summary: When you rotate the coordinate axes, the *numbers* used to describe a vector change — but the underlying physical/geometric objects do not.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- When you rotate the coordinate axes, the *numbers* used to describe a vector change — but the underlying physical/geometric objects do not. Check each: - **A vector $\vec{A}$ itself** — it is an arrow in space with a definite length and direction; it exists independently of how you choose to draw axes through it. Unchanged. ✓ - **Magnitude of a vector**, $|\vec{A}| = \sqrt{A_x^2+A_y^2+A_z^2}$ — this is a fixed geometric length (the diagonal of the box formed by the components), and rotating the axes redistributes $A_x, A_y, A_z$ among themselves while keeping this sum-of-squares the same. Unchanged. ✓ - **A scalar** (e.g. mass, temperature, speed) — by definition it is just a number with no direction, so it cannot depend on axis orientation at all. Unchanged. ✓ - **Component of a vector** — a component is the *projection* of $\vec{A}$ onto a chosen axis, $A\cos\theta$. As soon as you rotate the axis, $\theta$ changes and so does the component. This is the only one that *does* depend on orientation. So the things that do **not** depend on orientation are the scalar, the vector, and its magnitude — (a), (c), (d).
- For $\vec{C} = \vec{A}+\vec{B}$, the law of cosines gives the magnitude in terms of the angle $\theta$ between $\vec A$ and $\vec B$: $$C^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta$$ Take the case $A \le B < 2A$ (e.g. $A = 3,\ B = 5$). We want $C < A$ and $C < B$, i.e. $C < A$ (the smaller one is the binding condition). This requires $$A^2+B^2+2AB\cos\theta < A^2 \;\Rightarrow\; \cos\theta < -\frac{B}{2A}$$ Since $B < 2A$, we have $-\dfrac{B}{2A} > -1$, so such a $\theta$ (close to $180°$) genuinely exists. For instance with $A=3,\ B=5$: choosing $\theta = 170°$ gives $\cos\theta \approx -0.985 < -5/6 \approx -0.833$ ✓, and indeed $$C = \sqrt{9+25+2(3)(5)(-0.985)} = \sqrt{34 - 29.55} \approx 2.1$$ which is less than both $A=3$ and $B=5$. So it **is** possible to have $C < A$ and $C < B$ simultaneously (this happens whenever the vectors point in nearly opposite directions and partially cancel). The other options claim impossible absolutes ("always greater", "always equal to A+B", "never equal to A+B") which the triangle law contradicts. Answer: (B).
- By the law of cosines, with the angle between $\vec A$ and $\vec B$ equal to $120°$ (so $\cos 120° = -\tfrac{1}{2}$): $$C^2 = A^2+B^2+2AB\cos 120° = A^2+B^2 - AB$$ Compare this with $|\vec A - \vec B|^2 = A^2+B^2-2AB$: $$C^2 - |\vec A-\vec B|^2 = (A^2+B^2-AB)-(A^2+B^2-2AB) = AB$$ Since $A$ and $B$ are nonzero (positive) magnitudes, $AB > 0$, so $C^2 > |\vec A-\vec B|^2$, which gives $$C > |\vec A - \vec B| \quad \text{always (for this fixed } 120° \text{ angle)}$$ This is just the general triangle inequality $C > |A-B|$ becoming a *strict* inequality here because the angle is fixed at $120°$ (not $0°$, where equality would hold). Answer: (C).
- When several vectors $\vec{A}_1, \vec{A}_2,\ldots$ are added, vector addition works component-by-component, so the $x$-component of the resultant is exactly the algebraic sum of the individual $x$-components: $$C_x = \sum_i A_{i,x} = \sum_i A_i\cos\theta_i$$ This makes **(a) always true**. Now compare $C_x$ with $\sum_i A_i$ (the sum of magnitudes): - Since $\cos\theta_i \le 1$ always, $C_x = \sum_i A_i\cos\theta_i \le \sum_i A_i$. So $C_x$ can **never exceed** $\sum A_i$ — this rules out (c). - $C_x$ **equals** $\sum A_i$ only in the special case every vector points exactly along $+x$ (all $\theta_i = 0$, $\cos\theta_i = 1$) — so (d) is *possible*. ✓ - $C_x$ is **smaller** than $\sum A_i$ whenever any vector has a component along $y$ or points at an angle (some $\cos\theta_i < 1$, or vectors partially cancel along $x$) — so (b) is also *possible*. ✓ Hence (a), (b), (d) are correct, while (c) is impossible.
- The magnitude of the vector (cross) product is $$|\vec{A}\times\vec{B}| = AB\sin\theta$$ where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec A$ and $\vec B$. Since $0 \le \sin\theta \le 1$: - **Maximum** value is $AB$, reached when $\theta = 90°$ (perpendicular vectors) → so $|\vec A\times\vec B|$ **can equal** $AB$. ✓ (b) - For any other angle ($0° < \theta < 90°$ or $90° < \theta < 180°$), $\sin\theta < 1$, so the product is **less than** $AB$. ✓ (c) - When $\theta = 0°$ or $180°$ (parallel or antiparallel vectors), $\sin\theta = 0$, so the product is **zero**. ✓ (d) - It can **never exceed** $AB$, because $\sin\theta$ never exceeds $1$ — ruling out (a). So (b), (c), (d) are all genuinely possible values, while (a) is impossible.
- - **Strain** = change in length / original length = $[\rm L/L]$ = dimensionless ✓ - **Refractive index** = speed of light in vacuum / speed in medium = $[\rm LT^{-1}/LT^{-1}]$ = dimensionless ✓ - **Relative density** = density of substance / density of water = $[\rm ML^{-3}/ML^{-3}]$ = dimensionless ✓ - **Velocity gradient** = $dv/dx$: $[\rm LT^{-1}/L] = [\rm T^{-1}]$ — has dimensions of inverse time (same as angular velocity). ✗ Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- The argument of a sine function must be dimensionless (in radians): - $\omega t$: $[\omega][\rm T] = \rm dimensionless \Rightarrow [\omega] = T^{-1}$ ✓ (the product is dimensionless) - $\phi$ (phase constant): must also be dimensionless (in radians) ✓ - $A$ (amplitude): has dimensions of displacement $[\rm L]$ — not dimensionless ✗ - $\omega$ alone has dimensions $[\rm T^{-1}]$ — not dimensionless ✗ Answer: (A), (B).

formulas.latex: C > |\vec A - \vec B| \quad \text{always (for this fixed } 120° \text{ angle)}

title: Significant Figures
summary: Given that, length = 55.3 m breadth = 25 m As we know, resultant should have 2 significant digits.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Given that, length = 55.3 m breadth = 25 m As we know, resultant should have 2 significant digits. Area = length × breadth = 55.3 × 25 = 1382.5 m² = 13.8 × 10² m² ≈ 14 × 10² m²
- Value = 9.99 m - 0.0099 m = 9.9801 m Taking significant figure, both the values have two significant figures after decimal point so current answer is 9.98 m.

title: The International System of Units
summary: Radians and Steradians are measured in either $\pi$ (pi) or degrees.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Radians and Steradians are measured in either $\pi$ (pi) or degrees. Because $\pi$ and degrees are pure numbers. Planes and solid angles have no dimensions. Plane and solid angles both have units but no dimensions.
- Thermal conductivity, $$ \begin{aligned}&K=\frac{L}{A\Delta T}\frac{dQ}{dt}\\ &\quad=\frac{Metre}{(Metre)^{2}\times Kelvin}\times watt\\ &\quad=Wm^{-1}K^{-1}\\ \end{aligned} $$
- The value of the Stefan-Boltzmann constant is approximately $ 5.67 \times 10^{-8} $ watt per meter square per kelvin to the fourth of absolute temperature. (W.m $ ^{-2} $K $ ^{-4} $).

title: reNEET2026
summary: On an optical bench, the pole/centre of a lens or mirror does not coincide with the index pointer; this gap is corrected using index correction.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- On an optical bench, the pole/centre of a lens or mirror does not coincide with the index pointer; this gap is corrected using index correction.
- $[\sigma_s]=[M T^{-3}K^{-4}]$, $[k_B]=[M L^2 T^{-2}K^{-1}]$, $[b]=[L K]$. Then $\sigma_s k_B^{-1} b=[L^{-1}T^{-1}K^{-2}].$
